## CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL RULES

### NEVER hardcode stdlib/core type knowledge in the compiler

The compiler implementation (verum_types, verum_vbc, etc.) must have **ZERO knowledge** of
stdlib (`core/`) types. All type properties must be discovered from source declarations:

- **Affine types**: Discovered from `type affine Foo is { ... };` declarations. No hardcoded lists of types like `Heap`, `File`, `Mutex`, etc.
- **Variant constructors**: User-defined variant names (Nil, Cons, etc.) must freely override built-in convenience aliases. Only protect variants from ALREADY-REGISTERED types in the environment, never via hardcoded name lists.
- **Protocol implementations**: Never assume which types implement which protocols.
- **Type methods**: Never hardcode method availability on specific types.

**Why**: Hardcoding stdlib knowledge creates a circular dependency between the language
implementation and its standard library, making the language inexpressive and brittle.
Users and stdlib authors must be able to define any type with any semantics without
modifying the compiler.

**History**: This rule has been violated multiple times:
- `register_stdlib_affine_types()` hardcoded Heap, File, TcpStream, etc. as affine (DELETED)
- `protected_stdlib_variants` hardcoded None, Some, Ok, Err names (NEEDS FIX: should use registration order / first-registered-wins semantics instead)
- Various places assume `Result<T,E>` and `Maybe<T>` exist and have specific variants
